Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Dways International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Dways International Pte Ltd was incorporated in November 2018 in Singapore.
At incorporation, Nancy Long was a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran were shareholders.
Dways International Pte Ltd was formerly known as D’way International Pte Ltd and as Longevite Pte Ltd.
The OCBC Account was set up in November 2018 with Nancy and Zul as authorised signatories for cheques and Nancy as the sole authorised person to conduct internet-banking transactions.
On 8 March 2019, Irene became a director of Dways and the Lims became shareholders around the same period of time.
Where appropriate, Nancy, Zul and the Lims were referred to collectively as the Four Persons.
The Chuas were engaged as Dways' distributors and assisted with stock-taking and collection of Products by other distributors for the purpose of sales.

In February or March 2019, discussions among the Four Persons outlined that Irene would be the director in charge of accounts and operations and that Justin would be appointed as CFO to assist Irene and supervise the execution of her duties.
From early 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin participated in Dways International Pte Ltd's business operations as a substantial shareholder without a formal written employment contract.
Around mid-June 2019, the WhatsApp chats showed discussions pertaining to the name cards and the Four Persons' respective designations in Dways.
A document entitled 'Order of namecards' indicated the name and designation printed on each name card.
In July 2019, an invoice from a vendor was signed by Nancy Long as 'CEO' and by Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as 'CFO' on behalf of Dways International Pte Ltd.
In mid-March 2019, an unsigned partnership agreement was circulated stating that Irene would be the CFO and Justin the Chief Operating Officer.
From March 2019, numerous WhatsApp chat groups were created among the Four Persons and staff to discuss Dways International Pte Ltd's business and operations.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene took charge of making payments for Dways International Pte Ltd, maintained spreadsheets of payments to suppliers and vendors, and updated Nancy Long on bank balances.
From March 2019 to February 2020, the Lims had access to and control over the Bank Accounts, Products, records and inventory, while Nancy also retained access to the Bank Accounts and premises.

Until around November 2019, Nancy Long had joint control with Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene over the OCBC bank account of Dways International Pte Ltd, including receiving bank statements at her home until September 2019.
On 24 October 2019, a WhatsApp message from Karen Tang indicated that store and locker keys for Dways International Pte Ltd were kept in an office drawer.
On or about 25 November 2019, Nancy Long signed an OCBC form authorising any one director of Dways International Pte Ltd to apply for account and banking services.

On 20 December 2019, Nancy Long informed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene that she did not have the bank tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's accounts and requested online access; Irene replied that she took charge of accounts and would continue making payments while travelling.

By around 20 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene retained the internet banking tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts and was the only person effecting online transfers and payments.

On or about 25 November 2019, the authorised signatory for Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB bank account was changed to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as sole signatory, with Nancy Long signing a form authorising a single user to transact.

Both Nancy and Irene were authorised signatories of the UOB Account, and Nancy established that any internet-banking transaction had to be approved and performed by both of them until the signatory change in November 2019.
In October 2019, the mailing address for Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC bank account was changed from Nancy Long's home address to the company's office address.
On 8 November 2019, Karen Tang informed Nancy Long by WhatsApp to bring two sets of Dways International Pte Ltd office keys to be duplicated for new staff.
On 21 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd received stock of the product HL Span from its manufacturer.
On 1 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd received stock of the product Purity from its manufacturer.
On 17 November 2019, Dways received stock of B'Glo from its manufacturer, and Nancy instructed that it be delivered to the Lims' Home.
On 28 November 2019, staff member Carol informed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene by WhatsApp that Nancy Long had taken 12 boxes of Purity from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.
On 3 December 2019, Nancy Long took HL Span products from a cabinet at Dways International Pte Ltd's office and recorded the quantities later in the company's books.

On 21 December 2019, Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran took HL Span, Purity and B’Glo products from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock, with quantities later described in WhatsApp messages by Karen Tang.

Throughout 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd used a computer system to record distributor information, product purchases and amounts paid, accessible by Nancy Long, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene and staff.
During 2019, the Chuas conducted stock checks for Dways International Pte Ltd and placed stock records in office drawers.

On 6 January 2020, Karen Tang reported a shortfall of 17 boxes of HL Span in Dways International Pte Ltd's stock, and Nancy Long replied that she had taken them earlier and had written down what she had taken.

On 4 July 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd disbursed a loan of $30,000 to Karen Tang and Chua Hong Chor, with the loan described in a WhatsApp group titled 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' and a stated repayment period of four months.
On 4 July 2019, a message in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group recorded that Dways International Pte Ltd agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven and Karen with repayment in four months.
On 23 September 2019, in the 'DWay Accounts Updates' WhatsApp group, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene reported to the Four Persons that there was a loan amount of S$30,000 to Steven and Karen outstanding.

On 5 December 2019, Nancy Long messaged the Chuas stating that the outstanding S$30,000 loan from Dways International Pte Ltd was supposed to have been settled in October but remained unpaid in December.
On 6 January 2020, Nancy Long and Lim Kim Hwa Justin discussed repayment of the S$30,000 loan from the Chuas in WhatsApp messages.
On 1 April 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene emailed Nancy Long stating that since the S$30,000 was a Dways loan to the Chuas, Dways should pursue repayment from them.
On 28 July 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed a profit balance of approximately US$699 from an initial investment of S$10,500.

On 31 July 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed a profit balance of approximately US$829, with Justin messaging Nancy Long that the profit was US$829 in less than two weeks for the S$10,500 invested.
On or about 11 November 2019, a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed an accumulated profit balance of approximately US$1,527.20.
On 25 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd made a donation of S$1,500 to Psalt Care Ltd for a charity dinner, with the donation discussed in relation to using profits from the TLC investment.
On 1 August 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid S$1,500 to Zulkifli bin Othman Curran, with internal messages referring to using TLC investment profits to fund this payment.

On 20 and 21 July 2019, members of the 'DWay Office Reno' WhatsApp group, including Nancy Long and the Defendants, discussed procuring a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror for Dways International Pte Ltd's office and its cost.
On 31 July 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$11,676.75 for renovation works at its office, including S$2,900 for a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On or around 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$2,340, including S$1,580 for fabrication and installation of a mirror beside an existing folding door.

On 31 December 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid VSK Design Pte Ltd S$2,220 for works including a table cabinet beside a stage with an additional step and a slide-and-fold door with key lock at a downstairs storeroom.

On 30 August 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$6.80 at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, S$77.55 and S$81.10 at Tim Ho Wan, and S$24.00 at Au Croissant for meals and refreshments for persons assisting with office furniture installation.
On 14 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$20.22 at Crown Coffee for refreshments for the Four Persons.
On 13 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$75.21 at Tim Ho Wan for dinner for the Chuas who assisted in setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 2 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card was used to pay S$45.79 at Tim Ho Wan for meals for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$156.10 for refreshments for an Opportunity Product Presentation held on 14 September 2019.
On 23 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$530.46 for refreshments and meals for directors, shareholders and staff in or around August and September 2019.
On 30 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$230 for engaging a cleaning company to clean the company's premises in about September 2019.
On 4 September 2019, internal messages recorded that Dways International Pte Ltd required its shareholders to top up its accounts due to low balances.
On 27 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a UOB cheque for S$3,000 payable to Yang Tjun Yu, who had carried out website-related work for the company.
On 27 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a UOB cheque for S$1,045.45 payable to employee Norlin as part of her remuneration.
On 18 October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a cheque for S$3,959 payable to Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd for corporate merchandise or related services.
On 14 November 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd issued a cheque for S$3,664.75 payable to Stratgeist Pte Ltd for production of promotional videos.
On or about 30 October 2019, an ACRA business profile search for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd costing S$5.50 was conducted and later reimbursed from Dways International Pte Ltd funds.

Enhance Life was incorporated in October 2016 with Irene as its shareholder and director. Subsequently all Four Persons became its shareholders, and Nancy stated that she was a director from 11 June 2019 to April 2021.

In September and October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid CPF contributions for employee Norlin by transferring amounts of S$144 and S$178 to her, with the understanding that she would declare them to the CPF Board.

In October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd reimbursed Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene S$36,783.22 for various expenses she had paid on behalf of the company, including staff CPF contributions and other operational costs.
In October 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid season parking fees of S$192.60 for the month of October 2019 for Karen Tang, who was assisting substantially at the office.

On 9 January 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$10,210 from its UOB account to a DBS account held by Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, recorded internally as a payment to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.
On 15 January 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$400 from its UOB account to WLP Pte Ltd as accounting fees for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.
On 12 September 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid Cosmo System Pte Ltd S$911.64 for cloud server and website domain services.
On 4 December 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid Cosmo System Pte Ltd S$252.51 for cloud server and website domain services.

On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$4,276.84 to Karen Tang as commission and bonuses due to her as a distributor, after deducting a referral bonus related to a refund request by her downline Sandra.

On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$21,000 from its UOB account to an account of Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene, comprising S$10,500 for her and S$10,500 for Lim Kim Hwa Justin as allowances.

By about 17 January 2020, the working relationship between the Lim siblings and Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran had deteriorated, and a meeting was held where the possible sale of the Lim siblings' shares in Dways International Pte Ltd was raised.
On 22 January 2020, Nancy Long and Lim Kim Hwa Justin met to discuss the potential sale of the Lim siblings' shares in Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 23 January 2020, Lim Kim Hwa Justin went to Dways International Pte Ltd's office and removed products HL Span and Purity from the premises.
On 30 January 2020, the Lim siblings, assisted by the Chuas, went to Dways International Pte Ltd's office and removed products HL Span and Purity from the premises.

From around September or October 2019, the Four Persons operated on an understanding that each of them could take a monthly quantity of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo products from Dways International Pte Ltd for personal consumption and for distribution as samples.
From around late 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd used CCTV cameras in and around its office to record movements, including product removals.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether unrecorded allowance withdrawals, inter-company transfers, product removals, and an outstanding S$30,000 loan executed by company directors and shareholders constituted actionable misappropriation or were legitimate corporate actions governed by informal practices.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors and those exercising control over corporate assets are strictly bound by fiduciary duties to apply company funds exclusively for proper corporate purposes. They must document and justify all payments, ensure that any personal product entitlements are grounded in agreed arrangements, and guarantee that corporate loans or investment proceeds are properly repaid or accounted for. Diverting company funds or inventory without valid authorization or corporate benefit constitutes a breach of trust.
Party_Details.Application: Nancy Long and Zulkifli incorporated Dways. Irene and Justin Lim subsequently joined as shareholders and directors. Irene assumed control of the company's bank accounts and internet banking tokens. During this time, company funds were disbursed for various expenses, including a S$30,000 loan to the Chuas, external investments (which accrued profits), and operational costs. As the business relationship deteriorated in early 2020, the Lim siblings and the Chuas removed inventory from the office. Concurrently, Irene initiated a unilateral S$21,000 allowance transfer to herself and Justin. The core legal conflict is whether these specific outgoing payments, unreturned loans, and product removals were authorized and legitimate corporate actions, or whether they constituted actionable misappropriation and breaches of fiduciary duties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court held that while Irene had assumed primary responsibility for accounts and payments—making her a custodial fiduciary of the bank accounts—Nancy had voluntarily ceded day-to-day internet banking control. Dways failed to prove that the Lims had exclusive control over inventory and sales records, as a decentralized, honour-based system was utilized by all parties. 

Regarding the disputed payments, the court differentiated between legitimate operational expenses and unauthorized extractions. Payments for IT services, renovation works visibly installed at the office, CPF reimbursements, and reasonable meal expenses were upheld as valid commercial outlays. Conversely, the court found that the S$21,000 allowance withdrawal lacked any documentary support or prior approval; its execution amidst a deteriorating relationship constituted a breach of the no-profit and no-conflict rules by Irene, with Justin liable for conspiring in this specific withdrawal. Furthermore, payments linked to a separate entity (Enhance Life) and suspect distributor refunds routed through Irene's personal accounts were deemed unauthorized depletions of Dways' assets. 

The S$30,000 loan to the Chuas was clearly documented in WhatsApp communications as a corporate loan with a four-month repayment term. In the absence of any contemporaneous evidence of a write-off, the Chuas remain jointly and severally liable to repay the debt to Dways. Similarly, Irene and Justin are liable to account for the US$1,527.20 in accumulated TLC investment profits, as there was no evidence the funds were returned to the company. 

On product entitlements, the court recognized an informal 'Shareholders’ Entitlement' for personal consumption and sampling but rejected the sweeping 'Family Entitlement' asserted by the Lims as a retroactive justification. The product removals executed by the Lims and the Chuas on 23 and 30 January 2020 exceeded their permitted monthly quotas and were therefore unauthorized. Consequently, liability was apportioned based on the specific unauthorized transfers, unreturned loans, and misappropriated inventory.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene

Facts.Text: On 8 March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became a director of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene served as a director of Dways International Pte Ltd and was internally described as being in charge of accounts and operations.
Around March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene managed Dways International Pte Ltd's bank accounts, prepared spreadsheets of payments to suppliers and vendors, and updated other shareholders on account balances.

From March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene created and administered numerous WhatsApp chat groups for Dways International Pte Ltd's business, including groups for accounts updates, office renovation, and loans.
Around end March 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene was added as an authorised signatory to Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC bank account.
On or about 25 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene became the sole authorised signatory for Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB bank account.
By 20 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene held the internet banking tokens for Dways International Pte Ltd's OCBC and UOB accounts and was the only person executing online transfers and payments.
In July 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene signed a vendor invoice on behalf of Dways International Pte Ltd using the designation 'CFO'.
In mid-March 2019, a draft partnership agreement identified Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene as Chief Financial Officer of Dways International Pte Ltd.

On 20 and 21 July 2019, in the 'DWay Office Reno' WhatsApp group, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene discussed with Nancy Long and others the installation and cost of a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror for the office.

On 31 July 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$11,676.75 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd for renovation works including a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.

On or around 23 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$2,340 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd, including S$1,580 for a mirror beside an existing folding door.

On 31 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged payment of S$2,220 from Dways International Pte Ltd to VSK Design Pte Ltd for a table cabinet, additional stage step, and a slide-and-fold storeroom door.
On 12 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused Dways International Pte Ltd to pay S$911.64 to Cosmo System Pte Ltd for IT services.
On 4 December 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused Dways International Pte Ltd to pay S$252.51 to Cosmo System Pte Ltd for IT services.
In September and October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene reimbursed herself S$144 and S$178 from Dways International Pte Ltd for CPF contributions she had paid directly to employee Norlin.
In October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene received a reimbursement of S$36,783.22 from Dways International Pte Ltd for various expenses she had incurred on its behalf.
On 30 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged reimbursement of S$230 from Dways International Pte Ltd to herself for cleaning services for the office.
On 23 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged reimbursement of S$156.10 and S$530.46 from Dways International Pte Ltd to herself for refreshments and meals related to company activities.
On 30 August 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay for meals at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, Tim Ho Wan, and Au Croissant for persons assisting with office work.
On 14 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$20.22 at Crown Coffee for refreshments for the Four Persons.

On 13 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$75.21 at Tim Ho Wan for dinner for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 2 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene used Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card to pay S$45.79 at Tim Ho Wan for meals for the Chuas who assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation.
On 27 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,000 to Yang Tjun Yu for website-related work.
On 27 September 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$1,045.45 to employee Norlin as part of her remuneration.
On 18 October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,959 to Global Premiums & Gifts Pte Ltd.
On 14 November 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Dways International Pte Ltd to issue a cheque for S$3,664.75 to Stratgeist Pte Ltd for promotional video production.

On or about 30 October 2019, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene conducted an ACRA business profile search on Enhance Life International Pte Ltd costing S$5.50 and later reimbursed this amount from Dways International Pte Ltd.
Since October 2016, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene has been a shareholder and director of Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 9 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$10,210 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to her DBS account, recorded internally as a payment to Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 15 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$400 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to WLP Pte Ltd for accounting services for Enhance Life International Pte Ltd.

On 1 February 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene caused S$21,000 to be transferred from Dways International Pte Ltd's UOB account to her personal account, allocating S$10,500 to herself and S$10,500 to Lim Kim Hwa Justin as allowances.
On 23 January 2020, Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene arranged for Lim Kim Hwa Justin to remove HL Span and Purity products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director who takes exclusive control over corporate bank accounts breaches fiduciary duties by executing unrecorded allowance transfers to herself and related parties, routing distributor refunds through personal accounts, and facilitating unauthorized product removals.
Party_Details.Rule: A director acting as a custodial fiduciary of company bank accounts is strictly liable to apply those funds exclusively for the company's benefit. Any disbursement of funds or extraction of inventory for personal or related-party benefit must be grounded in clear corporate authorization, formally documented, and untainted by conflicts of interest.
Party_Details.Application: From March 2019, Irene became a director and took control of Dways' bank accounts and internet banking tokens. She authorized routine payments to operational vendors, reimbursed herself for company expenses, and processed a S$30,000 loan to the Chuas. As the business relationship soured in early 2020, she authorized a S$21,000 allowance transfer to herself and Justin without a written agreement, routed purported distributor refunds through her personal accounts, and facilitated the removal of company products beyond the informally agreed quotas. The dispute hinges on whether Irene, as the director controlling the company's finances, breached her fiduciary duties by executing these specific unapproved allowances, facilitating questionable refunds, and permitting the product removals, or if these actions aligned with established informal corporate practices.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director who retains the sole internet banking tokens and executes online payments operates as a custodial fiduciary of the company's funds. While payments made to vendors for visible IT, renovation, and marketing services possess a legitimate commercial basis, transfers lacking documented corporate approval or distinct business justification constitute a breach of duty. Extracting a S$21,000 'allowance' amidst a deteriorating relationship, without prior written agreement or consistent historical practice, violates the no-profit and no-conflict rules. Similarly, transferring company funds to benefit a separate entity in which the director holds an interest, or routing unverified distributor refunds through personal accounts, reflects a pattern of unauthorized asset depletion. Furthermore, facilitating the removal of inventory beyond the mutually accepted personal consumption quota is an actionable misappropriation. Consequently, the director is personally liable to restore the funds and value of inventory extracted without legitimate corporate basis.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Kim Hwa Justin

Facts.Text: On or around March 2019, Lim Kim Hwa Justin became a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.
Justin participated in Dways International Pte Ltd's business operations to some extent informally but did not possess a formal written employment agreement as CFO.
In mid-March 2019, an unsigned partnership agreement stated that Irene would be the CFO and Justin would be the Chief Operating Officer.
Around mid-June 2019, discussions about name cards for the Four Persons began, and Justin's name card stated he was 'Founder and CFO'.
Justin participated in WhatsApp chat groups discussing Dways International Pte Ltd's business and operations.

On 28 July 2019, Justin sent Nancy a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showing a sum of US$699 and said that next week they could pay Zul S$1,500 without using company funds, instead withdrawing profit from TLC.
On 31 July 2019, Justin sent Nancy a screenshot of the TLC investment wallet showing US$829 and described it as 'Profit U$829 in less than 2 weeks for DWAY888 invested 10,500'.
On or about 11 November 2019, the TLC investment wallet for account DWAY888 showed an accumulated amount of US$1,527.20.
On 1 August 2019, there was a message stating that S$1,500 could be paid to Zul without using company funds, instead withdrawing profit from TLC.
In October 2019, a S$1,500 donation to Psalt Care Ltd for a charity dinner was made using company funds.
On 23 September 2019, in the 'DWay Accounts Updates' WhatsApp group, Irene stated that there was a loan amount of S$30,000 to Steven and Karen.
On 4 July 2019, in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group, Irene sent a message stating that Dways had agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven and Karen with repayment in four months.
On 6 January 2020, Nancy and Justin discussed the repayment of the S$30,000 loan from the Chuas by WhatsApp.
On 22 January 2020, Nancy and Justin discussed the sale of the Lims' shares to Nancy and/or Zul.
On 23 January 2020, Justin went to Dways' office and removed Products from the premises.
On 30 January 2020, Justin, together with Irene and assisted by the Chuas, removed Products from Dways' office.
On 1 February 2020, Irene caused S$21,000 to be transferred from Dways' UOB Account to her account, comprising her director's allowance of S$10,500 and Justin's shareholder's allowance of S$10,500.
From about September or October 2019, the Four Persons orally agreed that they would each be entitled to 12 boxes of HL Span, six boxes of Purity and six boxes of B’Glo per month.
On 5 February 2020, Justin ceased to be a shareholder of Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a substantial shareholder without formal banking control is liable for conspiracy or misappropriation by participating in unauthorized allowance withdrawals, retaining company investment profits, and executing unsanctioned product removals.
Party_Details.Rule: Shareholders and de facto officers who participate in company financial decisions must not collaborate in or benefit from transfers of company funds or inventory that lack a proper corporate basis. Assisting or agreeing to an unauthorized extraction of funds by a fiduciary constitutes an actionable conspiracy, rendering the participant jointly liable for the loss. Additionally, failure to account for or remit corporate investment profits is a breach of duty.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Kim Hwa Justin acted as a substantial shareholder and participated informally in Dways' business operations without a formal employment contract. He monitored the TLC investment wallet (which accrued US$1,527.20 in profits), was allocated S$10,500 from the S$21,000 allowance transfer initiated by Irene, and actively removed products from the company's premises alongside Irene and the Chuas during the relationship breakdown. The primary legal issue is whether Justin, acting as a substantial shareholder and de facto officer, bears liability for conspiring to misappropriate corporate funds via the allowance transfer, retaining unreturned investment profits, and conducting unauthorized product removals.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A shareholder who lacks direct control over the company's bank tokens is not a custodial fiduciary and cannot be held liable for the entirety of the company's disputed operational payments. However, liability for conspiracy to injure arises when that shareholder agrees to and directly benefits from a specific, unauthorized extraction of corporate funds, such as the unilateral S$21,000 allowance transfer executed during a period of corporate discord without documentary authorization. Furthermore, active monitoring and acknowledgment of corporate investment profits (such as the TLC wallet) trigger an obligation to account for those funds; failure to ensure their return to the company renders the shareholder jointly liable for the retained balance. Similarly, participating in the physical removal of inventory beyond the mutually agreed personal consumption quota constitutes unauthorized misappropriation. Consequently, liability is restricted to the specific transactions in which active participation, conspiracy, or direct benefit is proven.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tang Lee Cheng (Karen)

Facts.Text: In early 2019, Tang Lee Cheng, also known as Karen, was engaged as a distributor of Dways International Pte Ltd.
From early 2019, Karen assisted Dways International Pte Ltd with stock-taking and collection of products by other distributors.
During 2019, Karen assisted Nancy Long with preparing compensation plans for distributors, communication materials, and arranging appointments with vendors for Dways International Pte Ltd.
On 8 November 2019, Karen informed Nancy Long by WhatsApp to bring two sets of Dways International Pte Ltd office keys to be duplicated for new staff.
On 24 October 2019, Karen sent a WhatsApp message stating that store and locker keys for Dways International Pte Ltd were kept in an office drawer.
On 28 November 2019, Karen relayed to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene that Nancy Long had taken 12 boxes of Purity from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.

On 21 and 22 December 2019, Karen sent WhatsApp messages to Lim Seow Hui Ratna Irene listing quantities of HL Span, Purity and B’Glo taken by Nancy Long and Zulkifli bin Othman Curran from Dways International Pte Ltd's stock.
On 4 December 2019, Dways International Pte Ltd paid S$192.60 for season parking for Karen.

On 30 August 2019, meals for Karen and Steven while they were installing furniture at Dways International Pte Ltd's office were paid using the company's debit card at Ya Kun Kaya Toast, Tim Ho Wan, and Au Croissant.
On 13 September 2019, dinner for Karen and Steven at Tim Ho Wan, after they assisted with setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation, was paid using Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card.
On 2 November 2019, meals for Karen and Steven at Tim Ho Wan, after they assisted with an Opportunity Product Presentation, were paid using Dways International Pte Ltd's debit card.
On 1 February 2020, Dways International Pte Ltd transferred S$4,276.84 to Karen.
On 31 July 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$11,676.75 from Dways International Pte Ltd for renovation works at its office, including a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On or around 23 September 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,340 from Dways International Pte Ltd, including S$1,580 for a mirror beside an existing folding door.
On 31 December 2019, VSK Design Pte Ltd received S$2,220 from Dways International Pte Ltd for a table cabinet, additional stage step, and a slide-and-fold storeroom door.

On 4 July 2019, Irene sent a message in the WhatsApp chat group 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' stating that 'DWay has agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven Karen on 4 July 2019' with '[l]oan repayment in 4 months' time'.
During 2019, Karen conducted stock checks for Dways International Pte Ltd and placed stock records in office drawers.
On 30 January 2020, Karen assisted the Lims in removing products from Dways International Pte Ltd's office.
In February 2020, the Chuas ceased to be distributors for Dways International Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company loan disbursed to an operational assistant and distributor remains a valid, enforceable corporate debt in the absence of a documented write-off agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate loans disbursed to related parties or distributors must be repaid according to their agreed terms. A claim that a corporate debt has been forgiven or written off requires clear, contemporaneous evidence of mutual agreement by the company's authorized officers. Bare assertions of an unrecorded waiver are insufficient to discharge the repayment obligation.
Party_Details.Application: Karen acted as a distributor and operational assistant for Dways. She received a S$30,000 loan alongside her husband Steven from the company, with a documented four-month repayment term. No formal written record of a write-off was produced. She also received commissions, had season parking paid by the company, and assisted the Lim siblings in removing products from the office. The central conflict is whether the S$30,000 loan remains an outstanding, enforceable corporate debt or was legitimately discharged through an unrecorded write-off agreement, and whether her participation in the product removals renders her liable for misappropriation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: When a loan is explicitly documented in corporate communications with clear repayment terms, the burden falls on the borrower to prove that the debt was subsequently forgiven. A bare assertion that a director orally agreed to write off the loan during a meeting is implausible, particularly when the parties habitually utilize messaging applications to record significant corporate decisions and when subsequent messages continue to treat the loan as outstanding. Consequently, the undocumented write-off defense fails, and the borrower remains jointly and severally liable to repay the outstanding principal to the company. While certain operational reimbursements (like commissions and meals) were deemed legitimate due to direct approval and business context, the loan repayment obligation remains strictly enforceable.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 447 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chua Hong Chor (Steven)

Facts.Text: In February 2020, Steven ceased to be a distributor for Dways International Pte Ltd.
Steven assisted with stock-taking and collection of Products by other distributors for the purpose of sales.
VSK Design Pte Ltd was an entity owned by Steven and carried out renovation works for Dways.
On 31 July 2019, VSK Design was paid for renovation works for Dways’ office totalling $11,676.75, including $2,900 for the supply and installation of a four-panel aluminium frame with mirror.
On around 23 September 2019, VSK Design was paid $2,340 for three items, including $1,580 for the fabrication and installation of a mirror beside an existing folding door.

On 31 December 2019, VSK Design was paid $2,220 for items of work, including $1,130 for a table cabinet beside stage and additional step for stage, and $520 for a slide-and-fold door with key lock at the store room downstairs.

On 4 July 2019, Irene sent a message in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group stating that DWay had agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven Karen on 4 July 2019, with loan repayment in 4 months' time.
On 4 July 2019, Irene sent a message in the 'DWay Loan to Steven Karen' WhatsApp group stating that DWay had agreed to loan S$30,000 to Steven Karen.
On 30 August 2019, Dways' debit card was used for meals and refreshments claimed to be for the Chuas who were installing furniture at Dways' office.
On 13 September 2019, Dways' debit card was used for dinner for the Chuas who had assisted Dways in setting up an Opportunity Product Presentation conducted by Zul.
On 2 November 2019, Dways' debit card was used for meals purchased from Tim Ho Wan for the Chuas, who were assisting with Dways' OPP on that day.
On 30 January 2020, the Lims, with the Chuas' assistance, removed Products from Dways' office.
In February 2020, the Chuas ceased to be distributors for Dways.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a documented corporate loan extended to an independent contractor and distributor must be repaid when the borrower alleges an unrecorded oral write-off agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate loans must be repaid according to their expressly agreed terms. To successfully claim that a corporate debt has been waived, the borrower must produce credible, contemporaneous evidence of a mutual agreement by the company to discharge the debt. Uncorroborated oral assertions of a write-off cannot defeat written communications confirming the debt's existence.
Party_Details.Application: Steven, alongside Karen, received a S$30,000 loan from Dways with an agreed four-month repayment window. Concurrently, his company (VSK Design Pte Ltd) provided renovation services to Dways and received multiple payments. He also participated in removing products from the company office as the relationship between the founders and the Lim siblings collapsed. The central conflict is whether the unrecorded write-off of the S$30,000 loan is legally effective and whether the renovation payments were properly authorized by the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: Payments made to a related party's business for physical office renovations are legitimate corporate expenditures when supported by invoices, visibly completed works, and the tacit awareness or lack of objection from the company's directors. Conversely, a documented loan to that same party remains an enforceable corporate debt. A defense relying on an unrecorded oral waiver is implausible when the parties' standard practice involves documenting decisions via text messages, and subsequent communications explicitly refer to the loan as an outstanding obligation. Consequently, the loan is not discharged, and the borrower remains jointly and severally liable to repay the outstanding sum to the company.
